title: Eggs Benedict

overview.description: Toasted English muffins topped with ham, perfectly poached eggs and silky homemade Hollandaise sauce.

method: Melt the butter gently until just melted. Keep warm.
Whisk together the egg yolks and lemon juice in a heatproof bowl.
Place over a pan of barely simmering water.
Whisk constantly until the mixture thickens.
Slowly drizzle in the melted butter while whisking continuously.
Season with salt and cayenne, then keep warm.
Bring a saucepan of water to a gentle simmer and add the vinegar.
Crack each egg into a small bowl.
Create a gentle swirl in the water and carefully slide in the eggs.
Poach for approximately 3 minutes until the whites are set but the yolks remain runny.
Meanwhile, toast the English muffins and warm the ham.
Top each muffin half with ham, a poached egg and a generous spoonful of Hollandaise.

learning.did-you-know: Despite its name, Eggs Benedict is generally believed to have been created in New York in the late nineteenth century rather than in England.
learning.what-you-will-learn: How to make a stable Hollandaise sauce, poach eggs successfully and coordinate several cooking tasks to finish together.

notes.why-its-in-the-chapman-kitchen: A classic weekend breakfast that teaches several core cooking techniques in one rewarding dish.
chapman-tips: Fresh eggs hold together much better when poached.
Keep the poaching water just below a boil—vigorous bubbling will break the eggs apart.
If the Hollandaise becomes too thick, whisk in a teaspoon of warm water.
Have everything ready before poaching the eggs so they can be served immediately.
Crack each egg into a small ramekin or teacup first. It makes it much easier to slide the egg gently into the water and lets you check for any shell before cooking.
